posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
This is a phenomenal wine.

But fiirst, do not touch this wine for another five years minimum, and only with a long decant!

When we think of the unfortunate modernization of so many historic Chateau, this is not it. Montrose, in 2005, remains unabashedly classic, old school. This is a behemoth wine with no glossy trappings. It’s what one expects from an estate like Montrose in a year like 2005. Has a massive Cabernet profile, big bold dark cassis, hints of pencil cedar shavings, and earthy and leathery aromas and tobacco just starting to reveal. The mouthful is burly big ripe fruits with great tannic and acidic structure. Like having a fantastic, juicy seared ribeye with a little Pittsburg-style on it. I generally look askance at critics timing the finish, but damn does this wine go on forever. Decanted one hour, enjoyed it over a 2.5 hour dinner. A remarkable left bank wine. A wine of 50+ years easy.

(97 pts.)

posts.name: Mark Golodetz
posts.cooked: I remember this wine en primeur. Massive and very closed even then. I’d say five years is pretty liberal, as a recent note echoed yours. Perhaps 2030, perhaps longer. A wine you grandchildren will enjoy.

I’m a big fan of 2005 Montrose. That being said, I think 2003 is better and the 2009 is better still. It’s an extraordinary wine. Probably not for you, but I think it’s mind blowing stuff.

Crickey… At some point in time consumers taste the wines set the market price. They no longer pay based on older critics notes. This happens all the time elevating prices or pushing them down.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Terrific wine! We drank it recently alongside the 1989 and 1990, and I think it will prove to be better and more consistent than both at maturity.

Given the prevalence of brett-contaminated bottles among the 1989 and 1990, saying the 2005 would be more consistent is not necessarily high praise.

Yeah, and that is the big fly in the ointment for those vintages. 1990 for me is the better of the two, but more likely to be (for my brett threshold) catastrophically tainted. I had a brilliant bottle of 1989 this Spring in Champagne, but last week a lackluster and overtly medicinal bottle from perfect provenance over dinner in Beaune… so one’s mileage varies. Beyond any brett issues, to me the 1989 is a bit too overly low acid / high pH and as a result often ends up being somewhat flat; and to me, as the tannins melt, that tendency is more obvious. The 2005, however, is a truly brilliant wine!

posts.name: James Slawney
posts.cooked: 2005 BDX is such a lost, forgotten “great vintage of the century.” I remember buying 200 cases of it on the futures market for private consumption thinking the vintage would be the second coming of God, but quickly enough the fickle wine media industry turned its superlatives onto the quickly succeeding seductive, velvety 2009s and super-opulent 2010s as “even better than 2005” and just jettisoned 2005s as old-school classical tannin-ridden has beens. It really does not help matters that the best 2005 BDXs closed down pretty early and many have still not re-entered the world as living beverages–to the point that many Left Bank Chateau owners do not even include 2005s in any of their verticals. But occasionally there are indications that the patience and humility and forbearance of 2005 collectors will finally pay off.

posts.name: Chris_Rickey
posts.cooked:
Neal Martin noted in a recent article he did comparing 2000 to 2001 Bordeaux that he encountered a significant amount of 2000s that had brett, to such effect that he said the 2000 vintage may be the last of the “old-time” Bordeaux vintages.

NM: “I can draw a line that connects 2000 with previous vintages in the 1980s and 1990s. The 2000s have the same tropes. Some have not aged in what one might call “distinguished fashion,” and as Philippe Blanc mentioned, there is a touch of underlying greenness that one could (contentiously) argue is an element that many wine-lovers miss. There is undeniably a higher level of brettanomyces that I abide in small measures, but occasionally it comes at the cost of freshness and precision, certainly to a far greater degree than in 2001.”

posts.name: Mark Golodetz

posts.name: Chris_Rickey
posts.cooked:
Both 2014 and 2016 are “old time Bordeaux vintages” and also 2008, although at a lesser quality.

He wasn’t using the term as praise. In his retrospective, he liked the 2001 overall better than 2000.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Both 2014 and 2016 are “old time Bordeaux vintages” and also 2008, although at a lesser quality.

I think he is simply using “old time” to mean “bretty”, rather than classically balanced and structured, which is, I sense, how you’re using it.

posts.name: James Slawney
posts.cooked: Yes, that is the way I read it: Bretty horse farm = old time. Classic = balanced between fruit and structure (not over-ripe, etc.), not immediately accessible. In any case, 2001 was a sneaky vintage: it was right after the very hyped 2000, and I think alot of critics were reluctant to pull out the same superlatives for another “vintage of the century” one year after the last one. But in hindsight, many chateaux produced really nice 2001s and priced them at a level we can only dream about now, e.g. I remember buying cases of Poyferre 01 and Pontet Canet 01 and other really amazing classified growths for less than $40/ bottle. Oh, the good old days.

posts.name: Chris_Rickey
posts.cooked:
Both 2014 and 2016 are “old time Bordeaux vintages” and also 2008, although at a lesser quality.

I think he is simply using “old time” to mean “bretty”, rather than classically balanced and structured, which is, I sense, how you’re using it.

I think that is part of it, but I also think he was referring to the quality of the tannins. I got the sense from the article that he thinks many of the 2000s are burdened by overly-drying, clunky tannins, which points back to old-time winemaking techniques. That’s why when he said the 2001 vintage points to the future, he very specifically said not the Noughties, but the subsequent decade through now. He’s referring to tannin management techniques, both in the vineyards and in the winery (“careful” extraction being the current in-vogue term).

If I have seen a shift in post-Parker Bordeaux wine criticism, it seems to revolve around tannins and extraction. Parker liked tannic wines; he just also like there to be enough fruit to offset the tannins. I get the sense that the current crop of critics, particularly the British ones, don’t like tannins as much.

posts.name: Mark Golodetz
posts.cooked:
An interesting perspective; having tasted 2005 Bordeaux extensively both en primeur and some recently in bottle, I never sensed a lot of Brett. Tannic certainly, and not the fine ripe tannins of recent vintages, nor the massive ones of say 1986, but somewhere in between. Personally, I love the vintage, and those tannins. It reminds me of al dente pasta; just the right amount of fight in the wines.

Montrose has always been subject to Brett. I sold my 1990s because it was such a crapshoot, but the good ones were pretty amazing. The 2005 seems to have managed to avoid it.
ITB

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
An interesting perspective; having tasted 2005 Bordeaux extensively both en primeur and some recently in bottle, I never sensed a lot of Brett.

But the article that got us onto this tangent was talking about 2001 vs 2000 rather than 2005.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Both 2014 and 2016 are “old time Bordeaux vintages” and also 2008, although at a lesser quality.

I think he is simply using “old time” to mean “bretty”, rather than classically balanced and structured, which is, I sense, how you’re using it.

I think that is part of it, but I also think he was referring to the quality of the tannins. I got the sense from the article that he thinks many of the 2000s are burdened by overly-drying, clunky tannins, which points back to old-time winemaking techniques. That’s why when he said the 2001 vintage points to the future, he very specifically said not the Noughties, but the subsequent decade through now. He’s referring to tannin management techniques, both in the vineyards and in the winery (“careful” extraction being the current in-vogue term).

If I have seen a shift in post-Parker Bordeaux wine criticism, it seems to revolve around tannins and extraction. Parker liked tannic wines; he just also like there to be enough fruit to offset the tannins. I get the sense that the current crop of critics, particularly the British ones, don’t like tannins as much.

Ah, well, I think it’s difficult to generalize about wine making techniques, which vary from estate to estate, with such precision. One can talk about trends over a decade or two, but to pinpoint a shift in a single year, well, that’s tricky. And sure enough, there are some 2001s that are still very tannic today.

I think you’re right, though, that contemporary wine criticism is pretty tannin averse. That is amplified when wines are tasted in large tastings where tannin accumulation exaggerates the perception of how tannic wines are.

posts.name: Mark Golodetz
posts.cooked: Maybe I got this wrong, but the comment was that 2005 was the last of the “old fashioned vintages”, that somehow seemed code for Brett. Unless it was specifically referring only to Montrose, in which case, I got my wires badly crossed.

posts.name: Robert Alfert

Maybe I got this wrong, but the comment was that 2005 was the last of the “old fashioned vintages”, that somehow seemed code for Brett. Unless it was specifically referring only to Montrose, in which case, I got my wires badly crossed.

My comment about Neal Martin referred to 2000. Someone else said that he thought 2005 was an “old fashioned” vintage. In terms of brett, I don’t think anyone meant to suggest the 2005 Montrose had unusual levels of brett incidence.

posts.name: Chris_Rickey
posts.cooked:
If I have seen a shift in post-Parker Bordeaux wine criticism, it seems to revolve around tannins and extraction. Parker liked tannic wines; he just also like there to be enough fruit to offset the tannins. I get the sense that the current crop of critics, particularly the British ones, don’t like tannins as much.

Ah, well, I think it’s difficult to generalize about wine making techniques, which vary from estate to estate, with such precision. One can talk about trends over a decade or two, but to pinpoint a shift in a single year, well, that’s tricky. And sure enough, there are some 2001s that are still very tannic today.

I think you’re right, though, that contemporary wine criticism is pretty tannin averse. That is amplified when wines are tasted in large tastings where tannin accumulation exaggerates the perception of how tannic wines are.

I don’t think he was suggesting 2001 marked some kind of turning point in winemaking practices. I think he just meant, as a gross generalization of the kind you would find in the introductory article, that 2001 had more in common with more recent vintages than the 2000 vintage, which struck him as a kind of old-fashioned style of Bordeaux.

In terms of Montrose specifically, it is a marked counterexample to the broad brush I painted in my post. Neal Martin, for instance, rates Montrose very highly despite Montrose being a very structured and tannic wine, and Parker rated the 2005 Montrose consistently lower than Neal Martin because he had concerns about the tannins. Broad brushes have their uses, but aren’t always right when applied to specific cases.

posts.name: James Slawney

I picked up no Brett in the 2005, but then again, I’ve been accused of living in an outhouse in Loire.

Sorry for sidetracking the discussion. I have picked up no brett in Montrose 2005, but I have picked it up in some Right Bank 2005s (La Gaffeliere, Gracia, Certan de May, and some others).

posts.name: Larry Link
posts.cooked:
This topic spurred me to open a Montrose, my 05s are buried offsite so I opened an 03. It is in a good place, evolving nicely.

10/28/2021 rated 96 points: Decanted for 1 hour. Cedar, black currant, eucalyptus oil and some earth on the nose. The palate is surprisingly fresh, deep blackberry fruit, silky texture with some medium grained tannins. Somewhat austere for the vintage, this wine is built to last.

Is Montrose 2009 any good? Should I really open one already?

Sure, take one for the team.

Neal Martin has three notes on the 2009 from 2019, all 98-98+ (the second highest he gave a 2009 in his retrospective, after only the 2009 Latour, which he scored 98±99). All three notes indicate that it might be shutting down, but will be great in the long run. Typical note:

“The 2009 Montrose remains a backward Leviathan Saint-Estèphe at ten-years of age, a wine that is clearly going to age over many decades. It has an intoxicating bouquet with blackberry, briary, crushed stone and rose petals that just gains more and more intensity with aeration without losing one iota of precision. The palate is medium-bodied with very fine tannin, adorned with an unerring sense of symmetry and precision. It just seems to lacquer the mouth with intense fruit and leaves a persistent wake of mineral drenched black fruit. Incredible.”

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked:
Maybe I got this wrong, but the comment was that 2005 was the last of the “old fashioned vintages”, that somehow seemed code for Brett. Unless it was specifically referring only to Montrose, in which case, I got my wires badly crossed.

My comment about Neal Martin referred to 2000. Someone else said that he thought 2005 was an “old fashioned” vintage. In terms of brett, I don’t think anyone meant to suggest the 2005 Montrose had unusual levels of brett incidence.

I picked up no Brett in the 2005, but then again, I’ve been accused of living in an outhouse in Loire.

I thought you lived in an outhouse called the state of Florida and just visit the Loire.

posts.cooked: This thread + WK’s purchase on the other thread got me to taste an '05. I am no Bordeaux expert, but this is good stuff that lingered in memory and so I bought a bunch more of it. I posted it on the other thread as a wine to buy in '21. For me, it was deep red fruit, rose tinged flowers, pencil lead/smoke, with med+ acidity that wasn’t ‘too fresh’.

posts.name: Charlie Carnes
posts.cooked:
This is a phenomenal wine.

But fiirst, do not touch this wine for another five years minimum, and only with a long decant!

When we think of the unfortunate modernization of so many historic Chateau, this is not it. Montrose, in 2005, remains unabashedly classic, old school. This is a behemoth wine with no glossy trappings. It’s what one expects from an estate like Montrose in a year like 2005. Has a massive Cabernet profile, big bold dark cassis, hints of pencil cedar shavings, and earthy and leathery aromas and tobacco just starting to reveal. The mouthful is burly big ripe fruits with great tannic and acidic structure. Like having a fantastic, juicy seared ribeye with a little Pittsburg-style on it. I generally look askance at critics timing the finish, but damn does this wine go on forever. Decanted one hour, enjoyed it over a 2.5 hour dinner. A remarkable left bank wine. A wine of 50+ years easy.

(97 pts.)

Dammint! My last Montrose purchase was a sixer of 2000. Am I going to have to buy this… for my kids?
